title: Point-Set Topology
subject: topology
author: M Padraig McLoughlin << Michel Smith << W.S. Mahavier << R.L. Moore
abstract: These notes offer a general introduction to Point-Set Theory. The notes offer the student the opportunity to investigate limit points, closed or open sets, compactness, countability, sequences, continuity, connectedness, sseparability, local compactness, and total connectedness.

title: A First Course in College Calculus
subject: calculus
author: Tulsi Srinivasan
abstract: These notes are for a first course in college calculus/introductory real analysis. The first part of the course motivates the study of sequences and their convergence through problems involving calculating the area under a graph and the slope of the tangent at a point on a graph. The second part draws on observations from the first about structural differences between the reals and the rationals, and starts with some axioms for the reals. These axioms are then used to study continuity and properties of continuous functions on the reals.

title: Discrete Math (Introduction to Proof)
subject: discrete math
author: Rachel Schwell
abstract: This text covers a collection of standard topics in an introduction-to-proof course: logic, techniques of proof including induction, set theory, functions, and introductory number theory. Sets and functions (including injectivity and surjectivity) are given special attention, in preparation for upper-level courses such as abstract algebra. The text is intended to be standalone in an IBL setting with no supplementary lecture or notes required and is scaffolded accordingly. It is meant to be user-friendly, while still encouraging readers to stretch their thinking and develop mathematical habits of mind.

title: The Story of Statistics: Inquiry based notes for introductory statistics
subject: statistics
author: Lindsey Bell, Nicholas Pritchard, and Douglas Weathers
abstract: Notes for an introductory statistics course emphasizing the process of statistical thinking. Topics include data collection, graphical and numerical summaries, introduction to probability, random variables, sampling distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and simple linear regression. A common investigation is woven throughout the notes to provide continuity and hands on activites are used to introduce new conepts.

title: Finite Mathematics
subject: finite math
author: Mindy Capaldi
abstract: These notes are for a four-credit, 100-level finite mathematics course. Topics include matrix algebra, linear programming, probability, counting methods, Markov chains, and game theory. In practice, studuents work through the notes during class, complete problems in groups, and present their work at the board. Two modules using board games are also included as appendices. The notes can be easily adapted by taking out explanatory passages or examples, based on the mathematical background of the students.

title: Precalculus: An Inquiry-Based Approach
subject: precalculus
author: Howard, Paul K. < Mahavier, W. Ted
abstract: A standard precalculus course covering polynomials, rationals, exponentials, logarmithmics, trigonometry, polar coordinates, rate of change, and conic sections. This course may also serve as either College Agebra or Trigonometry simply by choosing the appropriate chapters.

title: Mathematical Logic for the Humanities
subject: logic
author: Ryan P. Dunning
abstract: A mathematics course designed for students in the humanities. A variety of concepts are covered. Topics will be drawn from the following areas: introductory treatments of sets, logic, number theory.

title: Complex Variables and Functions
subject: analysis (complex)
author: Bowman, Joshua P.
abstract: The material covered in these notes is standard for an undergraduate course on complex variables. The vast majority of exercises (“tasks”) are original, however. A few tasks are borrowed or adapted from exercises or exposition

title: Linear Algebra: Notes and Problems
subject: linear algebra
author: Long, Nicholas
abstract: This set of notes and problems covers a junior-level linear algebra course (following an introduction to proof course) with broad chapter topics of 1) eciently solving systems of linear equations, 2)vector spaces, 3) connecting ideas (dimension, invertibility, and eignvalues), and 4) innner product spaces. The approach of these notes isa blend of computational and sense-making problems, as well as an increasing usage of conjecture and proof throughout the course.

title: Introduction to Combinatorial Games
subject: game theory
author: Gantner, Ryan
abstract: Through this course, students will build a robust theory of combinatorial games from scratch, including theories of partisan games, impartial games, nimbers versus numbers, the \mex" function, and basic Sprague-Grundy theory. Several games will be introduced, such as Nim, Hackenbush, Kayles, and Chomp. These notes are aimed at students who are not math majors and who have not taken college math courses previously. With only slight modication, however, this could become a course for math majors.

title: An Inquiry-Based Learning Approach to Mathematical Modeling
subject: modeling
author: Young, Andrea
abstract: This is a set of notes for a one semester course in Mathematical Modeling. The topics covered are difference equations, Markov chains, Monte Carlo simulations, and linear programming. The notes emphasize scientific computing and include both problems and projects. These notes are written to be used with Sage, but they could be modified to incorporate another language.

title: Calculus I – Single Variable Calculus
subject: calculus
author: George, C. Yousuf; Lewis, Heather A.
abstract: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of calculus, its notation and its applications. Some of these concepts include: functions, rates of change, limits, derivatives, applications of derivatives, antiderivatives, integration, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

title: Discrete Mathematics
subject: discrete math
author: Clark, David M.; Mahavier, W. Ted
abstract: This course constitutes a mathematically rigorous introduction to sets, functions, counting, induction, equivalence relations, recursion, elementary graph theory finite state machines and languages.

title: Precalculus
subject: precalculus
author: Howard, Paul K. < Mahavier, W. Ted
abstract: This course is designed to prepare students to begin a college level calculus series. Topics in this course include all aspects of trigonometry as well as conic sections, exponential functions, polynomial functions, rational functions and linear functions with an emphasis on rate of change. Strong emphasis is placed on strengthening understanding of algebraic concepts necessary to be successful in a college level calculus series.

title: An Inquiry-Based Introduction to Proofs
subject: foundations
author: Hefferon, Jim
abstract: An introduction to mathematical proof. Covers elementary number theory including divisibility and primes, sets including DeMorgan's laws, functions including one-to-one and onto, and relations. There is a brief optional chapter on cardinality. The presentation is a sequence of statements of definitions and results, with a few remarks.

title: One-Semester Real Analysis
subject: analysis (real)
author: Crisman, Karl-Dieter
abstract: This set of course notes consists of a course covering standard topics in real analysis, including accumulation points, sequence convergence, continuity, differentiation, and integration. It begins with some set and logic review, and presupposes some previous introduction to proof. In order to ensure culmination in a single semester with the Fundamental Theorem(s) of Calculus, more overtly topological concepts are elided.

title: Euclidean Geometry: An Introduction to Mathematical Work
subject: geometry
author: Hitchman, Theron
abstract: Planar Euclidean Geometry including triangles, polygons, circles, area, and constructions with a compass and straightedge. Emphasis is placed on the role of definitions and proving theorems via the axiomatic method.

title: An Introduction to Order Theory
subject: lattice theory
author: French, Zack; Hart, James
abstract: These materials provide an introduction to the basic concepts of partially ordered sets. Topics covered include fundamental definitions, Zorn’s Lemma, DCPO’s, classes of lattices, adjunctions and closure operators, compact generation, ideals, and irreducible elements.

title: Introduction to Point-Set Topology
subject: topology
author: Fuchs, Shay
abstract: These notes serve as the primary source for a one-semester (12 weeks) IBL course on Point-Set Topology. They cover standard material, such as: Open and closed sets in a topological spaces, closure and interior, continuous functions, quotient spaces, connectedness and compactness. It is assumed that students are familiar with the Euclidean space R^n, and some basic calculus. Background in Real Analysis is not required, but will help motivate some of the notions.

title: Introduction to Advanced Mathematics
subject: foundations
author: Baker, B.J.
abstract: An introduction to logic and methods of proof with applications to basic set operations, relations, functions, binary operations, and the real number system including mathematical induction and the completeness axiom.

title: Point-Set Topology
subject: topology
author: Clark, Lisa Orloff
abstract: Topology is one of the cornerstones in the foundation of modern mathematics. It appears everywhere. The purpose of this course is to give students the background in topology that they need to pursue higher level mathematics. Point-Set Topology is a very abstract in nature. The entire subject is built from a few set theoretic definitions that can be used in a wide variety of situations. In this course, we cover: Topological Spaces, basis , the product topology, continuity and homeomorphism, the quotient topology, metric spaces, compactness, separability, and connectedness.

title: Topology Notes
subject: topology
author: Person, Laura J.
abstract: This traditional treatment of general topology includes metric spaces, bases, separations properties, countability properties, compactness, continuity, connectedness, path connectedness, quotient spaces, the product topology, Tychonoff’s Theorem, Urysohn’s Lemma, Tietze’s Extension Theorem, the fundamental group, and covering spaces.

title: Calculus I: Notes and Problems
subject: calculus
author: Stallmann, Cornelius
abstract: This problem sequence is intended for an inquiry based first semester course in calculus. It introduces the student to differentiation using simple motion and tangent line examples. Differentiation rules are developed by the student. Optimization is investigated. The student discovers integration and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus through area explorations. Initial value problems are investigated as applications of anti-differentiation. The calculus of trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions as well as some of their applications are introduced. Basic anti-differentiation rules and techniques are discovered by the student. Some version of these notes was used by the author for ten semesters from 2002 through 2011 at Augusta State University (now Georgia Regents University).

title: Discovering Infinity
subject: set theory
author: Olinick, Michael
abstract: Infinity has intrigued poets, artists, philosophers, musicians, religious thinkers, physicists, astronomers, and mathematicians throughout the ages. Beginning with puzzles, paradoxes and the infamous Hilbert Hotel problems that show the need for careful definition and rigorous thinking, readers examine the idea of infinity within mathematics, discovering their own theorems and proofs about the infinite. The material is suitable for first year undergraduates and introduces them to elementary set theory, well-ordering and mathematical induction, functions, set equivalence, finite and infinite sets, countable and uncountable collections, the rational and real numbers, and infinity and dimension.

title: Calculus I, II, & III : A Problem-Based Approach with Early Transcendentals
subject: calculus
author: Mahavier, W. Ted
abstract: These notes constitute a self-contained sequence for teaching, using an inquiry-based pedagogy, most of the traditional topics of Calculus I, II, & III in classes of up to 40 students. The goal of the course is to have students constantly working on the problems and presenting their attempts in class every day, supplemented by appropriate guidance and input from the instructor.

title: Advanced Calculus with Generalizations: Second Semester
subject: analysis (real)
author: Vallin, Robert W.
abstract: This book covers the second semester of a two-semester Advanced Calculus/Real Analysis course for undergraduates. The topics covered include differentiation, integration, series of numbers, and series of functions.

title: Notes for a course on Financial Mathematics
subject: financial mathematics
author: Jensen-Vallin, Jacqueline
abstract: These notes are for a course on Financial Mathematics for non-majors. They are intended to be used with some instructor lecture or direction, and then with students working in groups. Topics covered include simple interest, compound interest, and ordinary annuties, but in enough depth to form the basis of a semester-long course.

title: Advanced Calculus with Generalizations: First Semester
subject: analysis (real)
author: Vallin, Robert W.
abstract: This book covers the first semester of a two-semester Advanced Calculus/Real Analysis course for undergraduates. The topics covered include Properties of Real Numbers, Limits, Sequences, Functions, and Continuity. There is a balance of statements which are true and need proofs and false and need counter-examples.

title: Foundations of Analysis
subject: analysis (real)
author: Mahavier, W. Ted << Mahavier, W.S.
abstract: A one-semester course covering analysis restricted to the real line. Limit points, sequences, continuity, differentiability and Riemann integration are covered, culminating in the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Emphasis is on the techniques of analysis as opposed to memorization or application of the theorems.

title: Mathematics for Elementary Teachers: Real Numbers
subject: mathematics education
author: Jones, Matthew G.
abstract: A one-semester course in a two-semester sequence required of students who intend to teach at the elementary level. The course includes numeration; the operations of addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division in the decimal system; integers; rational numbers; decimal fractions; proportions; percent; algebra arising from linear patterns. This course is about learning fundamental concepts that comprise the elementary school curriculum.

title: Differential Calculus
subject: calculus
author: Loft, Brian M.
abstract: These notes are designed for use in a 15-week, 4-credit-hour differential calculus course with students from both mathematics and the sciences. While a strong background in algebra is of course preferred, few assumptions about student knowledge of trigonometric, exponential and logarithmic functions are made. All preliminary concepts are thoroughly defined throughout the chapters, and the development of these definitions are meant to be an integral part of their use within differential calculus.

title: Linear Topology
subject: topology
author: Mahavier, W. Ted < Mahavier, W.S.
abstract: This is a traditional treatment of the topology of the line, addressing limit points, least upper bound, greatest lower bound, sequence convergence, Cauchy sequences, compactness, connectedness, and elementary measure theory.

title: Lebesgue Measure And Integration
subject: analysis (graduate)
author: Read, Tom
abstract: An inquiry-based learning approach to graduate real analysis that can be used for either a one- or two-semester course. The notes cover material that may be found in portions of Royden’s “Real Analysis,” Kolmorgorov and Fomin’s “Introduction to Real Analysis,” and Hewitt and Stromberg’s “Real and Abstract Analysis.”

title: Exploring Affine Transformations
subject: linear algebra
author: May, Jr., E. Lee
abstract: Building on a foundation of linear algebra – and, for the later chapters, real analysis -- this course introduces students to the realm of nonlinear transformations by means of affinities, which are linear translations plus a nonzero shift. Intended for advanced undergraduates and graduate students. The notes can be used for a class of several students, undergraduate or graduate, or as a guide for independent study.

title: Notes for a Course on Proofs
subject: foundations
author: Jensen-Vallin, Jacqueline A.
abstract: These notes are used for an introduction to proofs course including the following topics: logic, number theory, set theory, induction, and relations. In particular, the purpose of these notes is to help students learn how to critically examine their proofs and those presented by their classmates so that all students leave the class with a working knowledge of how to complete direct proofs, proofs by contrapositive, proofs by contradiction, and proofs by induction.

title: Introduction to Abstract Algebra
subject: group theory
author: Morrow, Margaret L.
abstract: A carefully sequenced collection of problems for an inquiry-learning approach in a one-semester Introduction to Abstract Algebra Class. The topics developed include basic notions about groups and subgroups; cyclic groups; homomorphisms and isomorphisms; and cosets. The notes culminate with the concept of quotient groups and the first isomorphism theorem. The approach is fairly elementary. A student with just a little experience with sets, functions, and elementary proof writing will be able to make substantial independent progress through the notes, particularly with the support of peers or instructor in an inquiry-based learning environment.

title: Theory of Computation
subject: automata theory
author: Ajwa, Iyad A.
abstract: A one semester undergraduate course in Theory of Computation focusing on the theory of finite state automata and context-free languages.

title: Mathematical Modeling
subject: modeling
author: Miller, Nathaniel
abstract: These course notes are designed to be used for a one-semester project-based course in mathematical modeling. They have been used for an online Master's level course for in-service secondary school teachers, as well as for a face-to-face upper-level capstone course for undergraduates. The course consists of eight interrelated projects in mathematical modeling.

title: Notes for R H Bing's Plane Topology Course
subject: topology
author: Miller, Nathaniel << Creswell, Sam << Bing, R H
abstract: These are notes for a one or two semester introductory point set topology class, originally authored by R H Bing, edited by Sam Creswell and then by Nathaniel Miller, and with teaching notes added by Nathaniel Miller.

title: Hilbert Geometry: A Guided Inquiry Approach
subject: geometry
author: Clark, David M.
institution: State University of New York at New Paltz
abstract: Axiomatic development of neutral geometry from Hilbert’s axioms with emphasis on a range of different models. Designed for a one semester IBL course.

title: Calculus III
subject: calculus
author: Mahavier, W. Ted
abstract:
These materials constitute a one semester course in multivariate calculus intended as the third semester of a three semester course, beginning with the definition of Euclidean three space and concluding with Green's Theorem in three dimensions.
Note: This issue has been superceded by another version. Please refer to Calculus I, II, & III : A Problem-Based Approach with Early Transcendentals, No. 37 (Apr. 2015) for a more recent version of this work.

title: A Do-It-Yourself Introduction to Number Theory
subject: number theory
author: Priestley, William << Cross, James T.
abstract: Largely an algebraic study of the standard number–theoretic functions, congruences, primes, quadratic residues, etc., taught by a modified Moore-method involving student presentations and interactions. The notes are intended for a semester-length junior level course for mathematics majors as an introduction both to number theory and to the basic ideas of abstract algebra.

title: Modern Geometry II
subject: geometry
author: Miller, Nathaniel
abstract: These are notes for a second semester class that explores Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometries from multiple perspectives, with an emphasis on developing problem solving, communication, and logical reasoning skills, directed primarily at pre-service secondary math. The non-Euclidian geometries include taxicab, hyperbolic, and spherical geometry. This is an inquiry-based class in which students spend most of their time in class working on problems in groups and then write them up. The materials are meant to be accompanied by the book Experiencing Geometry [Henderson & Taimina, 2005].

title: An Introduction to Probability and Statistics
subject: statistics
author: Clark, Lisa Orloff
abstract: These notes are a self contained inquiry based treatment of elementary probability and statistics. This includes a basic introduction to data analysis, descriptive statistics, correlation, Venn diagrams, probability, Bayes' Rule, random variables, probability distributions, confidence intervals, and statistical inference.

title: Modern Geometry I
subject: geometry
author: Miller, Nathaniel
abstract: These are notes for a class that explores Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometries from multiple perspectives, with an emphasis on developing problem solving, communication, and logical reasoning skills, directed primarily at pre-service secondary math teachers and pre-service elementary teachers specializing in math. It is an inquiry-based class in which students spend most of their time in class working on problems in groups and then write them up. The materials are loosely based on those in the book Experiencing Geometry [Henderson & Taimina, 2005].

title: Euclidean and Non-Euclidean Geometries
subject: geometry
author: Coppin, Charles A.
abstract: Development of the mathematical way of thinking through firsthand experience. Emphasis on the student’s strengthening of his or her imagination, deductive powers, and ability to use language precisely and efficiently. Study of Euclid’s geometry; Hilbert’s axioms; neutral geometry; hyperbolic geometry (non-Euclidean geometry of Gauss, Bolyai, Lobachevsky); and the axiomatic method. Historical perspective and philosophical implications are included. Students must prove a significant number of theorems on their own.

title: A First Course in Undergraduate Complex Analysis
subject: analysis (complex)
author: Spindler, Richard
abstract: Complex number system, polar form, analytic functions, differentiation and integration, series, residues.

title: Linear Point Set Theory
subject: topology
author: Coppin, Charles A.
abstract: Topology of the real line: limit points, convergent sequences, compact sets, connected sets, dense sets, nowhere dense sets, separable sets.

title: Analysis
subject: analysis (real)
author: Neuberger, John W.
abstract: These notes constitute a two-semester first undergraduate course in analysis. Someone who has a good understanding of the material in these notes has a good grounding in calculus, and a good start on advanced calculus. However, instructing students in this material has never been a top priority in my classes. The top priority has been to help students gain an ability to do mathematics for themselves -- to learn to discover arguments and to solve problems.

title: Analysis
subject: analysis (real)
author: Mahavier, W. Ted < Mahavier W.S.
abstract: These notes contain sufficient material for a two-semester course in undergraduate real analysis and have been in use in some form for more than forty years. The notes require no prerequisites. The beginning problems are elementary to develop mathematical maturity by proving theorems about limit points and sequences. After addressing continuity and differentiability, we address the standard theorems of calculus through the Fundamental Theorem. Uniform continuity, uniform convergence, and the existence and uniqueness of solutions to differential equations are treated in the second semester along with some elementary set and measure theory.

title: Foundations of Calculus
subject: calculus
author: Ingram, W. T.
institution: Missouri University of Science and Technology
abstract: A study of properties of the real numbers, functions, and continuity.

title: Linear Algebra
subject: linear algebra
author: Clark, David M.
institution: State University of New York at New Paltz
abstract: One semester undergraduate course (omitting last chapter) or beginning graduate course (omitting first chapter) in linear algebra: vectors in 3-space, structure of finite dimentional linear spaces and inner product spaces, linear transformations.

title: Advanced Calculus I and II
subject: analysis (real)
author: Beaver, Scott F.
abstract: Provides an Inquiry-Based Learning approach to the theoretical underpinnings of differential and integral calculus, in the setting of two 10-week terms. The course proceeds as students use definitions, axioms and previous theorems to prove new theorems. A variety of examples are provided to help augment students’ concept images. Sequences and series of both real numbers and of real-valued functions are treated rigorously, generating a sufficient prerequisite knowledge base from which to prove the basic foundations of calculus.

title: A Moore Method Calculus II Course
subject: calculus
author: Mahavier, William S.
abstract: This is an introduction to mathematical proofs in which students learn to speak and write with the accuracy required to communicate mathematical work effectively. It is a chance to learn what mathematics is really like. The course content is largely that of a calculus 2 course including what is in the BC version but not in the AB Advanced Calculus Course. The focus is on Riemann integrals, log and exponential functions, series and sequences. Students will be assigned problems to be done and presented in class. It is a course for students who like problems and don't like lectures.

title: Fraction Model for the Numbers
subject: foundations
author: Parker, G. Edgar
abstract: These notes create a model, starting with the set of ordered pairs of natural numbers, from which the algebraic and geometric axioms for the number continuum can be proven. No pre-requisite is assumed.

title: Place-Value Model for the Numbers
subject: foundations
author: Parker, G. Edgar
abstract: These notes create a model in the set of functions from N into {0,1} from which the algebraic and geometric axioms for the number continuum can be proven. No pre-requisite is assumed.

title: Discovering Properties of Real Numbers and Functions
subject: analysis (real)
author: Zettl, Anton
abstract: Students will discover and prove properties of real numbers and functions by presenting their own proofs and ideas in class. In this way, they will not only examine numbers and functions from fresh points of view, but also learn to use language precisely, make logical arguments and present these to a group.

title: Introduction to Proof
subject: foundations
author: Taylor, Ron
abstract: These notes are designed to be used to introduce students to the techniques and structure of mathematical proof. The intent is to build upon the foundation laid by calculus to prepare students for abstract mathematics courses.

title: Theory of Groups
subject: group theory
author: Clark, David M.
institution: State University of New York at New Paltz
abstract: A two semester undergraduate course in abstract algebra focused primarily on groups. Basic properties, cyclic groups, LaGrange's Theorem, homomorphisms, isomorphisms, representation theorems, normal subgroups and quotient groups. Many examples. Other algebraic systems.

title: Graphs & Optimization
subject: graph theory
author: Clark, David M.
institution: State University of New York at New Paltz
abstract: A freshman level general education course in the elementary theory of graphs and practical graph theoretic optimization problems: Euler paths and Euler circuits, the Traveling Salesman Problem, spanning trees, scheduling problems and planar graphs.

title: Trigonometry
subject: trigonometry
author: Mahavier, W. Ted
abstract: Topics addressed in this course include the unit circle, definitions and properties of the six trigonometric functions and their inverses, graphs of these functions, identities associated with these functions, the law of sines, the law of cosines, and applications.
